note: Sloping pointed majuscule | Contrast between thick and thin strokes; presence of serifs at the ends of thin strokes; the vertical strokes of 'rho', 'ypsilon', 'phi' end with a curve to the left; 'beta' is maximized and has round curves; the oblique strokes of kappa are detached from the vertical stroke; the central strokes of mu are merged into a curve; omega has rounded curves; angle of inclination ca. 118°

note.value: The text of this section of the palimpsest presents many differences from the Assemani edition; references to the Assemani edition do not always have a precise equivalent. The missing text between fols. 173v and 180r (Assemani 47 F 4-48 F 6) must have occupied the central bifolio of the quire
Description by Pasquale Orsini

Bibliograpy: J. S. Assemani (ed.), Sancti Patris Nostri Ephraem Syri opera omnia quae exstant graece, syriace, latine in sex tomos distributa. Vol. I, Graece et latine, Romae 1732, pp. 40-70 ; A. Hjelt (ed.), Syrus sinaiticus, Helsingfors 1930, p. 15 ; S. J. Voicu, Frammenti di un palinsesto greco di Efrem (Sinaiticus Syr. 30, f. 171 e seguenti), Scriptorium 38 (1984), pp. 77-78
Reconstructed folio order, ff. 178r, 178v, 174v, 174r, 173r, 173v, 180r, 180v, 179v, 179r, 175r, 175v

The UTOs of Ps. Marcarius/Symeon, Epistula magna (ff. 171, 172, 176, 177, 181, 182) and Ephraem Syrus, Sermo asceticus (ff. 173, 174, 175, 178, 179, 180) were copied by the same scribe and were part of the same original codex

note: Biblical majuscule | Similar to Vindob. theol. gr. 31, Paris. suppl. gr. 1286, and Codex Purpureus Rossanensis, suggesting a date in the sixth century (501-600 CE) and a place of origin in the region of Syria or Antioch (cf. G. Cavallo, Ricerche sulla maiuscola biblica (Florence, 1967), pp. 98-104; P. Orsini, Manoscritti in maiuscola biblica. Materiali per un aggiornamento (Cassino, 2005), pp. 176-177, 201-203, 210-211

note.value: These folios preserve two sections of text (John 6:68-7:31 and 8:53-9:23), with an extensive gap between them (John 7:31-8:52). The missing text would have occupied about 18 columns (9 sides, i.e. 4 folios + 1 side). Codicologically more likely is the exclusion of the “Pericope Adulterae” (7:53-8:11). In that case, the missing text would have occupied about 16 columns (8 sides, i.e. 4 folios.). These four lost folios were the two central bifolios of the quire (if it was a quaternion)
Description by Pasquale Orsini

Bibliography: R. L. Bensly, J. R. Harris, and F. C. Burkitt, The Four Gospels in Syriac Transcribed from the Sinaitic Palimpsest (Cambridge, 1894), p. xvi ; Α. S. Hjelt (ed.), Syrus sinaiticus, Helsingfors 1930, p. 15 ; C. Guignard, “0323: A Forgotten 4th or 5th Century Greek Fragment of the Gospel of John in the Syrus Sinaiticus,” Novum Testamentum 57 (2015), pp. 311-319

note: Sloping pointed majuscule | Contrast between thick and thin strokes; presence of serifs at the ends of thin strokes; the vertical strokes of rho, ypsilon, and phi end with a curve to the left; beta is maximized and has round curves; the oblique strokes of kappa are detached from the vertical stroke; the central strokes of mu are merged into a curve; omega has rounded curves; angle of inclination ca. 118°

note.value: About 52 lines, corresponding to three sides of text (one folio + one side), of the Jaeger edition are missing between 271.16 (fol. 176v) and 274.2 (fol. 182r). About 39 lines, corresponding to one folio, of the Jaeger edition are missing between 281.13 (fol. 171r) and 283.6 (fol. 177r); these correspond to two sides (one folio) of text. These gaps coincide with a lost bifolio (the second in the quire, if this was a quaternio) and a lost page (the unreadable side of fol. 182 = pastedown); the original quaternio can be reconstructed as follows: I (176rv), II (fol. lost), III (recto unreadable, verso = pastedown/182r), IV (181vr), V (172vr), VI (171vr), VII (fol. lost), VIII (177rv)
Description by Pasquale Orsini

Bibliography: W. Jaeger (ed.), Two rediscovered works of ancient Christian literature: Gregory of Nyssa and Macarius (Leiden, 1954) ; A. Hjelt (ed.), Syrus sinaiticus (Helsingfors, 1930), p. 15 ; S. location.repository: St. Catherine's Monastery of the Sinai

note.value: The presence of quire signatures in Georgian, probably by a binder, points to the fact the manuscript was probably in Palestine between the 9th and the 10th c. CE
Pencil foliation beginning with '4', intending to include exposed unnumbered front pastedown as f. 1
Reinforced leather. Large parts missing in front; back cover in two vertical parts, reattached with thread; exposed spine
Mostly unbound quires
Most folios severely cockled, with raised ridges appearing as glossy; various degrees of discoloration, at least in part due to application of liquid in the course of prior scholarly investigation
Quires are established according to natural movement of folios and do not correspond to original Georgian quire signatures in overtext
Unbound quires until f. 150; large number of singletons at the beginning and end of quires (usually quinions) that may originally have been bifolios belonging to a quire
This is the well-known Codex Sinaiticus Syriacus
